sentences.grammarNote: 제 (je) is the polite/formal form of 'my'. The casual form is 내 (nae). Use 제 with strangers or in polite contexts; 내 with close friends.
To say nationality or origin, Korean uses the pattern [country] + 사람 (person) + 이에요/예요. There is no word for 'from' — the structure is literally 'I am an America-person'.

sentences.romanized: Jeoneun hangugeo reul bae ugo isseoyo.
sentences.grammarNote: The -고 있어요 (-go isseoyo) ending forms the present progressive tense: 배우고 있어요 = 'am learning'. It attaches to any verb stem and literally means 'doing and existing'.
어렵다 (eoryeopda) is an adjective verb meaning 'to be difficult'. It conjugates like a verb: 어려워요 in polite speech. Korean adjectives don't need a separate 'to be' — they are predicates on their own.

sentences.base_language: But it's also a lot of fun.
sentences.grammarNote: 재미있다 (jaemiitda) literally means 'fun exists' — 재미 (fun/interest) + 있다 (to exist/have). Its opposite is 재미없다 (jaemieopda) = boring. This pattern applies broadly: 맛있다 (delicious), 맛없다 (tasteless).

연습하다 (yeonseuphada) = to practice — 연습 (練習, practice) + 하다. 매일 (maeil) = every day — does NOT take the time particle 에; it stands alone as an adverb. 연습해요 = polite present tense of 연습하다 — 하다 verbs take 해요 in polite speech.

sentences.base_language: I watch Korean dramas to improve.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)려고 means 'in order to / with the intention of': 키우려고 = 'in order to develop'. 실력 (sillyeok) = skill/ability level — a key word in Korean self-improvement culture. 키우다 (kiuda) = to raise/grow/develop — used for children, animals, and skills alike.

sentences.base_language: My Korean is still not very good.
sentences.grammarNote: 서툴다 (seotulda) = to be clumsy/unskilled — an honest, self-deprecating way to describe language ability. 아직 (ajik) = still/yet — often used with negatives or incomplete states. 는 = topic marker after a vowel: 한국어는 sets 'my Korean' as the topic being commented upon.

포기하다 (pogihada) = to give up — 포기 (抛棄, abandonment) + 하다. -지 않다 is the long negation form: 포기하지 않아요 = 'do not give up'. It sounds slightly more formal or emphatic than 안 포기해요. 그래도 (geuraedo) = 'even so / nevertheless' — a key connector for expressing persistence.

sentences.base_language: Do you also want to practice Korean with me?
sentences.grammarNote: 저랑 (jeorang) = 'with me' — 저 (I, polite) + 랑 (casual 'with/and'). 같이 (gachi) = together — can be placed before or after 저랑. -고 싶어요? as a question = 'do you want to?' — rising intonation turns any statement into a question in Korean.

같이 배워요! = 'let's learn together!' — the polite present form 배워요 doubles as a 'let's' suggestion in context. 배우다 (baeuda) = to learn — stem 배우-, so polite form is 배워요. In Korean, there is no separate 'let's' form in casual speech; the context and same ending make it a shared invitation.
